title: Market structures
summary:
## The big idea Why does a wheat farmer take whatever price the market offers, while a patented-drug maker names its own? The answer is **market structure** — the competitive landscape a firm inhabits. Structures are classified by four features: **how many firms**, **how high the barriers to entry**, **how similar the products**, and **how good the information**. Every firm, whatever its structure, still profit-maximises at **MC = MR**; what differs is the *demand curve it faces* — and therefore the price, profit and efficiency that result. ## Perfect competition — the benchmark **Assumptions:** many small firms; **homogeneous** (identical) products; **no barriers** to entry or exit; perfect information. Each firm is a **price taker**: its output is a drop in the ocean, so it faces a **horizontal AR = MR** at the market price. - **Short run:** at MC = MR a firm can earn **supernormal profit** (price above AC) or make a loss. - **Long run:** profit attracts entry → market supply rises → price falls until every firm earns just **normal profit** (AR tangent to AC at its minimum). Losses cause exit and the reverse. - **Efficiency verdict:** in long-run equilibrium the firm produces at **minimum AC (productive efficiency)** and where **P = MC (allocative efficiency)** — the theoretical gold standard. The catch: with only normal profit and identical products, there is little money or motive for innovation — **dynamic efficiency is weak**. ## Monopoly — the other pole A **pure monopoly** is a single seller (in practice, dominant market power; some jurisdictions treat ~25%+ share as a working threshold). What sustains it are **barriers to entry**: economies of scale, patents and licences, control of key resources, brand loyalty, predatory conduct. The monopolist faces the **market demand curve as its AR**, with **MR below it**. Profit-maximising at MC = MR gives a **higher price and lower output** than a competitive industry with the same costs — and because barriers block entry, **supernormal profit persists into the long run**.
**What each part means:** the monopolist sets output **Qm** where **MC = MR** (yellow dot) and charges **Pm**, read vertically up on the **AR (demand)** curve (orange dot). A perfectly competitive industry with the same costs would instead settle where supply (MC) meets demand: the grey dot at **Qc, Pc** — more output, lower price. Consumers between Qm and Qc valued the good above its marginal cost yet go unserved: the **shaded orange triangle** is the **deadweight welfare loss** of monopoly, alongside a transfer of consumer surplus into monopoly profit.
**The efficiency verdict:** allocatively inefficient (**P > MC**), usually not at minimum AC, and sheltered enough to breed **X-inefficiency**. **But the defence matters:** where scale economies are vast, one big firm may produce *more cheaply* than many small ones (**natural monopoly** — water, rail track); and persistent supernormal profit can *fund R&D* — Schumpeter's argument that monopoly delivers the **dynamic efficiency** perfect competition can't. **Price discrimination** — charging different consumers different prices for the *same* product (peak/off-peak fares, student discounts). Requirements: market power, separable groups with **different elasticities**, and **no resale**. Effects: converts consumer surplus into profit (bad for those charged more), but can *raise total output* and let low-elasticity groups cross-subsidise service for price-sensitive ones — sometimes markets exist only because of it (evaluation gift). ## Monopolistic competition — the crowded high street **Many firms, differentiated products, low barriers**: cafés, hairdressers, restaurants. Differentiation gives each a *slightly* downward-sloping AR — mild price-setting power. - **Short run:** supernormal profit possible. - **Long run:** easy entry copies success until **AR is tangent to AC** — only **normal profit**, and at an output below minimum AC: **excess capacity**. Neither productively nor allocatively efficient, but consumers do get **variety**. ## Oligopoly — the strategic few A **few large firms** dominate (measured by the **concentration ratio** — e.g. the largest 4 firms holding 70% of sales). The defining feature is **interdependence**: each firm's best move depends on rivals' reactions, which is why oligopoly has *models* rather than one diagram. - **Kinked demand curve:** rivals match your price *cuts* (so demand below the current price is inelastic) but ignore your *rises* (demand above is elastic) → the kink makes MR discontinuous → **price rigidity**: prices stay put even when costs wobble. Firms compete instead on **non-price** fronts: branding, advertising, quality, loyalty schemes. - **Collusion:** interdependence tempts firms to stop competing — a **cartel** fixing price/output like a shared monopoly (OPEC; illegal for firms in most countries), or subtler **tacit collusion** such as **price leadership** (everyone follows the biggest firm). - **Game theory** — the **prisoner's dilemma** — explains both the temptation and the fragility: each firm's dominant strategy is to cheat (undercut), so cartels collapse without enforcement; repeated interaction, small numbers and punishment strategies make collusion likelier. ## Contestable markets — a different lens What disciplines a firm may not be the rivals it *has* but the entrants it *fears*. A market is **contestable** when entry and exit are cheap — crucially, **low sunk costs**. Then even a lone incumbent must price modestly, or 'hit-and-run' entrants will grab the profit. Policy moral: sometimes the best competition policy is *lowering entry barriers*, not counting firms. ## Why it matters Compare structures on **price, output, profit and the four efficiencies** and you have the engine of every Paper 4 firm essay — and the analytical basis for the competition policy of Unit 8.
## ⚡ Quick revision - Classify by: number of firms, barriers, product differentiation, information. All maximise profit at **MC = MR**. - **Perfect competition**: price taker, AR = MR; long run → **normal profit**, productive + allocative efficiency, weak dynamic. - **Monopoly**: barriers → long-run supernormal profit; **P > MC**, DWL, X-inefficiency risk — vs natural monopoly & dynamic efficiency defence. - **Price discrimination**: power + separable markets + different PEDs + no resale. - **Monopolistic competition**: differentiation, easy entry → long-run normal profit with **excess capacity**; variety. - **Oligopoly**: few firms, **interdependence**; kinked demand → price rigidity → non-price competition; cartels vs prisoner's dilemma. - **Contestability**: low sunk costs → threat of entry disciplines incumbents — behaviour over structure.

keyTerms.definition: Many price-taking firms with identical products and no barriers to entry; long-run normal profit.
A price taker accepts the market price; a price maker can influence price via a downward-sloping AR.
A single (or dominant) seller protected by high barriers to entry, earning long-run supernormal profit.
Obstacles (scale, patents, brand, sunk costs) that prevent new firms entering a market.
An industry where economies of scale mean one firm can supply the whole market at lowest cost.
Charging different prices to different consumers for the same good, needing market power and no resale.
Many firms with differentiated products and low barriers; long-run normal profit with excess capacity.
A market dominated by a few interdependent firms with high barriers to entry.
Firms agreeing to fix prices or output to act like a monopoly.
A market where low entry/exit costs mean the threat of entry disciplines incumbents.

diagrams: **Perfect competition:** horizontal AR=MR; short-run supernormal profit/loss vs long-run normal profit (AR=AC tangency).
**Monopoly:** downward AR, MR below it; MC=MR output; shade supernormal profit and the deadweight welfare loss vs the competitive (P=MC) output.
**Monopolistic competition long run:** AR tangent to AC at MC=MR output → normal profit with excess capacity.
**Oligopoly kinked demand curve:** demand kinked at the current price with a discontinuous MR → price rigidity.
**Price discrimination:** two sub-markets with different elasticities, each priced at its own MC=MR.
tips: Learn each structure by its **four assumptions** (number of firms, barriers, product, information) and its **long-run profit and efficiency** outcome.
Always draw the **deadweight welfare loss** on the monopoly diagram and compare with the competitive P = MC output.
Give a **balanced monopoly evaluation**: allocative/productive inefficiency vs economies of scale, natural monopoly and dynamic efficiency.
For oligopoly, use **interdependence + the kinked demand curve** to explain price rigidity, and **game theory/prisoner's dilemma** for collusion.
Bring in **contestability** to argue that *behaviour* (threat of entry) can matter more than the number of firms.

keyConcepts.title: Oligopoly, kinked demand and game theory

revisionNotes:
# Market structures — quick revision

**The comparison table**
| | Firms | Barriers | Product | LR profit | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Perfect competition | many | none | identical | normal | productive + allocative ✓, dynamic ✗ |
| Monopolistic comp. | many | low | differentiated | normal | excess capacity; variety |
| Oligopoly | few | high | either | supernormal possible | depends (collusion vs rivalry) |
| Monopoly | one/dominant | very high | unique | supernormal | P > MC, DWL, X-ineff.; dynamic? |

**Key mechanics**
- All: output at **MC = MR**; price off AR.
- Perfect competition long run: entry/exit → AR tangent to AC minimum.
- Monopoly: MR below AR; compare with P = MC point → **deadweight loss** triangle.
- Monopolistic competition long run: tangency *left of* AC minimum → **excess capacity**.
- Oligopoly: **interdependence**; kinked demand (match cuts, ignore rises) → rigid prices, non-price competition; **prisoner's dilemma** → why cartels form *and* collapse.

**Monopoly evaluation (both sides!)**
- Against: P > MC, lower output, X-inefficiency, DWL.
- For: economies of scale / **natural monopoly**, profits fund R&D (**dynamic efficiency**), price discrimination can extend the market.

**Contestable markets** — low sunk costs → hit-and-run threat disciplines even a monopolist; policy should target entry barriers.

**Price discrimination conditions** — market power, separable groups, different PEDs, no resale.
questions:
# Practice questions — Market structures **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** In long-run equilibrium, a firm in perfect competition earns: - A — supernormal profit, because entry is blocked - B — normal profit only, because entry competes profit away - C — a loss, because price equals marginal cost - D — zero revenue
▸ Show answer

B. Supernormal profit attracts entrants, raising supply and cutting price until AR is tangent to AC — normal profit only (which, remember, is a return counted in costs).

**2.** A monopoly is allocatively inefficient because at its profit-maximising output: - A — price exceeds marginal cost - B — price equals marginal revenue - C — average cost is minimised - D — it earns only normal profit
▸ Show answer

A. Setting MC = MR with MR below AR leaves P > MC: consumers value extra units above their production cost, yet they aren't produced — the deadweight loss.

**3.** The kinked demand curve model predicts that oligopoly prices are rigid because firms expect rivals to: - A — match both price rises and price cuts - B — match price cuts but not price rises - C — match price rises but not price cuts - D — collude on all price changes
▸ Show answer

B. Raise your price and you're alone (elastic demand above the kink — big sales loss); cut it and everyone follows (inelastic below — little gain). Either move loses, so price stays put.

**4.** Which condition is **not** required for successful price discrimination? - A — the ability to prevent resale between groups - B — different price elasticities of demand between groups - C — some market power over price - D — identical marginal costs in all markets
▸ Show answer

D. Costs may differ or not — irrelevant to the definition. What's essential is power, separable groups with different PEDs, and no arbitrage (resale).

## Paper 4 style — data response > Two firms share 85% of a country's mobile-phone market; three small rivals and an active regulator make up the rest. For five years the two giants' prices have moved in near lockstep, and neither has cut prices despite falling equipment costs. Both spend heavily on advertising and 'loyalty bundles'. When the regulator auctioned new spectrum to a foreign entrant, both incumbents cut prices within a month. **(a)** Define 'oligopoly'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain **two** pieces of evidence in the passage that suggest interdependent behaviour. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse, using the kinked demand curve, why prices stayed rigid while costs fell. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether this market needs further intervention by the competition authority. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A market dominated by a few large interdependent firms behind high entry barriers (85% two-firm concentration ratio here). [1+1]

(b) Prices moving in lockstep (possible tacit collusion/price leadership); heavy non-price competition (advertising, bundles) instead of price cuts; the instant price response to entry shows each firm watches rivals. Any two, explained. [4]

(c) Kink at the ruling price: rivals match cuts (inelastic below) but not rises (elastic above) → discontinuous MR → a fall in MC can move within the MR gap without changing the profit-maximising price. Diagram expected. [6]

(d) For: parallel pricing + rigid prices despite falling costs suggest consumers are overpaying; concentration is extreme. Against: parallel prices aren't proof of collusion (could be competition); non-price benefits exist; entry (spectrum auction) already disciplined prices — the market may be **contestable** enough. Options: more spectrum entrants, banning lock-in clauses, investigation rather than price caps. A justified judgement either way. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why a firm in perfect competition can earn supernormal profit in the short run but not in the long run. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the view that monopoly is always against the consumer's interest. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Short-run MC = MR with price above AC; no barriers → entry → supply rises → price falls to tangency (normal profit). Diagrams of both runs.

(b) Against consumers: P > MC, restricted output, DWL, X-inefficiency, less choice. For: natural monopoly scale economies → possibly lower prices than fragmented supply; dynamic efficiency from funded R&D (pharma); price discrimination can extend access. Judgement: depends on the industry's cost structure, contestability and regulation.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with reference to the prisoner's dilemma, why firms in an oligopoly may collude and why such collusion often breaks down. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether oligopoly is necessarily a less desirable market structure than monopolistic competition. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Joint profit is maximised by cooperation (cartel = shared monopoly), but each firm's dominant strategy is to cheat (undercut) → collusion is tempting yet unstable; repetition/punishment can sustain it. A payoff matrix earns credit.

(b) Oligopoly vices: collusion risk, high barriers, persistent supernormal profit. Virtues: scale economies, big R&D budgets (dynamic efficiency), fierce non-price innovation. Monopolistic competition: variety and easy entry, but excess capacity, small inefficient firms, little R&D. Judgement: neither dominates; depends on conduct (colluding vs competing oligopoly) — and contestability.
